subject: Science
q:
The speed of the transpiration stream varies with ______.
I.   light intensity
II.  temperature
III. wind and humidity
skill: Describe absorption of water in plants through roots
feedback: The speed of the transpiration stream varies with the surrounding conditions and the plant. Factors like light intensity, temperature, and humidity all affect the rate at which transpiration occurs.

subject: Science
q: Water lost in the leaves is replaced by drawing more water from the _____.
skill: Describe absorption of water in plants through roots
feedback: Plants constantly absorb and lose water. Water absorbed by the xylem is lost through the leaves. This entire transport system is called the transpiration stream.

subject: Science
q: The tiny pores on the underside of leaves are called ______.
skill: Describe absorption of water in plants through roots
feedback: Water is lost by the plant once it reaches the leaves. The leaves have tiny pores on the underside, called stomata, which allows water to diffuse and then evaporate.

subject: Science
q: Water is lost through _____ in the leaves.
skill: Describe absorption of water in plants through roots
feedback: Once water reaches the leaves, it evaporates and diffuses out of tiny pores on the underside of leaves.

subject: Science
q: Water and dissolved minerals are transported quickly up the xylem to the stem and leaves. The main force that draws water up the plants is a process called ______.
skill: Describe absorption of water in plants through roots
feedback: After entering the root hairs and xylem, water is driven by transpiration to the rest of the plant and leaves.

subject: Science
q: If ______ were the only method of taking in substances from soil, plants would not be able to differentiate between what substance is harmful or not.
skill: Describe absorption of water in plants through roots
feedback: Diffusion is a process that occurs automatically due to a change in concentration. Diffusion is used, along with osmosis and active transport, to take in water and nutrients by plants.

subject: Science
q: Like osmosis, diffusion does not need _____ to work.
skill: Describe absorption of water in plants through roots
feedback: Osmosis and diffusion are both natural processes and occur automatically when the conditions are right thus do not needing any energy input.

subject: Science
q: If you place a drop of ink in water without stirring it, the process by which the ink will spread is called _____.
skill: Describe absorption of water in plants through roots
feedback: Diffusion is a physical process that drives two solutions in contact to the same concentration. Therefore, ink wants to spread to areas with lower concentration in the water.

subject: Science
q: Mineral salts enter through a process called diffusion and are eventually carried up the ______.
skill: Describe absorption of water in plants through roots
feedback: Salts eventually reach the xylem vessels through a process called diffusion. The xylem vessel then transports it further to the stems and leaf veins.

subject: Science
q: Some mineral salts enter the root hairs through a process called ______.
skill: Describe absorption of water in plants through roots
feedback:
Some mineral salts enter the root hairs through a process called diffusion.

Diffusion is the spreading out of a substance from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration.

subject: Science
q: Osmosis occurs until the solution inside a root hair and the soil are of the same _____.
skill: Describe absorption of water in plants through roots
feedback: Osmosis occurs because of a difference in concentration. The solution inside a root hair becomes lower in concentration until water reaches the xylem vessels.

subject: Science
q: A surface that lets some molecules through but not others is called a ______.
skill: Describe absorption of water in plants through roots
feedback: A membrane has tiny holes that lets small water molecules pass through but not larger ones. This type of membrane is called a semi permeable membrane.

subject: Science
q: The process of taking water through root hairs is called ______.
skill: Describe absorption of water in plants through roots
feedback: Plants take in water through a process called osmosis. This is process when a solution moves from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration through a semi permeable membrane.

subject: Science
q: Behind the root cap are thousands of tiny tubes called _____.
skill: Describe absorption of water in plants through roots
feedback: Root hairs grow between the soil particles and are responsible for taking up water from the soil.

subject: Science
q: At the tip of a root, there are several layers of cells that form the _____. These cells are replaced quickly as the tip pushes through the soil.
skill: Describe absorption of water in plants through roots
feedback: The root cap is the tip of a root that pushes the root through the soil. These cells are replaced quickly.

subject: Science
q: Label the following diagram of the cross section of a leaf.
options: A (palisade cell), B (spongy cell), D (guard cell), E (stomata)
A (spongy cell), B (palisade cell), D (guard cell), C (epidermis)
F (palisade cell), B (spongy cell), D (stomata), C (epidermis)
A (palisade cell), B (spongy cell), D (stomata), F (cuticle)
skill: Describe the transport of water in plants
feedback: The correctly labelled diagram is given below. The upper layer of cells that contain more chloroplasts are known as palisade mesophyll cells, also simply called palisade cells. The lower layer of cells that have spaces between them are known as spongy mesophyll cells. They provide air spaces where water can evaporate and exit through the stomata. The stomata are an opening in the epidermis of the leaf and is regulated by two guard cells. The epidermis is covered by a waxy layer called cuticle which prevents water loss from the surface of the leaf.

subject: Science
q: If guard cells were present in a hypotonic environment, what would be the effect on the rate of transpiration?
options: The rate of transpiration would increase.
The rate of transpiration would decrease.
skill: Describe the transport of water in plants
feedback: In a hypotonic environment, the water would travel into the guard cells, making them turgid. This would increase the size of the stomata, thus increasing the rate of transpiration as well.

subject: Science
q: What is depicted by the arrows in the diagram below?
skill: Describe the transport of water in plants
feedback: The diagram shows that the substance escapes from the vascular bundle and enters the mesophyll cells from which it travels to the outside of the plant via the stomata. This is the route taken by water during transpiration. Carbon dioxide would be entering the leaf, not exiting it. Oxygen does exit the plant from the stomata, but it does not enter the leaf from the vascular bundle.

subject: Science
q: In which of the following temperature ranges would the rate of transpiration be the highest?
skill: Describe the transport of water in plants
feedback: For every 10 °C increase in temperature, the transpiration rate roughly doubles. However, when the temperature increases above 40 °C, the stomata close to prevent excess water loss. Therefore, the transpiration rate would be highest before the temperature becomes too high. The correct answer is 30-40 °C.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following correctly describes the route a water molecule would take during transpiration?
skill: Describe the transport of water in plants
feedback: Plants absorb water molecules from the soil via root hair cells. These water molecules make their way to the cortex of the vascular bundle and reach the xylem. From the xylem, they travel upwards, providing water to the aerial parts of the plant along the way. More than 90% of the water exits the vascular bundle and enters the leaf, travelling between mesophyll cells. The water molecules exit the cells and evaporate, exiting the leaf from the stomata in the form of water vapour. Therefore, the correct order is root hair cell→cortex→xylem→mesophyll cell→stomata.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following cells are directly responsible for controlling the rate of transpiration?
skill: Describe the transport of water in plants
feedback: Transpiration occurs through tiny openings called stomata. The size of the stoma is regulated by a pair of cells known as guard cells. If the guard cells are turgid, they assume a concave shape that opens the stoma. As the guard cells become flaccid, the stoma becomes smaller and eventually closes.

subject: Science
q: Most of the transpiration in plants primarily occurs through the _____.
skill: Describe the transport of water in plants
feedback: Stomata are the primary sites for transpiration which are present on the leaf's surface. They are the microscopic openings or pores in the epidermis of the leaves. They consist of two guard cells that control the opening and closing of stomata. Plants may also transpire from the stems through pores called lenticels, but this is only a small amount compared to stomata.

subject: Science
q: Which factor affects the rate of transpiration as shown below?
skill: Describe the transport of water in plants
feedback: The graph shows that as the factor 'X' increases, the rate of transpiration initially increases and then becomes constant. X cannot be humidity as it has an inverse relationship with the rate of transpiration. If X was temperature, then after a certain point, the temperature would exceed 40-50 °C causing the stomata to close and the transpiration rate to drop. Sound has no effect on the rate of transpiration; therefore, X can only be wind speed. Wind blows away humid air which increases the rate of evaporation of water from inside the leaf, increasing transpiration.

subject: Science
q: Which factor affects the rate of transpiration as shown below?
skill: Describe the transport of water in plants
feedback: The graph shows that as the factor 'X' increases, the rate of transpiration increases up to a certain point after which it starts to decrease again and eventually stops completely. This pattern is similar to what would be observed if the temperature was increased. Initially, an increase in temperature causes an increase in transpiration, but as the temperature reaches around 40 - 50 °C, the stomata close and transpiration stops.

subject: Science
q: Which factor affects the rate of transpiration as shown below?
skill: Describe the transport of water in plants
feedback: The graph shows that as the factor 'X' increases, the rate of transpiration decreases. Out of the given options, only humidity has such a relationship with transpiration. Humidity causes the air to become saturated with water, so that the air does not have the capacity to accept any more water vapours. This lowers the rate of evaporation and therefore, transpiration.

subject: Science
q: At the tip of a root, there are several layers of cells that form the _____. These cells are replaced quickly as the tip pushes through the soil.
skill: Describe the transport of water in plants
feedback: The root cap is the tip of a root that pushes the root through the soil. These cells are replaced quickly.

subject: Science
q: Behind the root cap are thousands of tiny tubes called _____.
skill: Describe the transport of water in plants
feedback: Root hairs grow between the soil particles and are responsible for taking up water from the soil.

subject: Science
q: The process of taking water through root hairs is called ______.
skill: Describe the transport of water in plants
feedback: Plants take in water through a process called osmosis. This is process when a solution moves from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration through a semi permeable membrane.

subject: Science
q: A surface that lets some molecules through but not others is called a ______.
skill: Describe the transport of water in plants
feedback: A membrane has tiny holes that lets small water molecules pass through but not larger ones. This type of membrane is called a semi permeable membrane.

subject: Science
q: Osmosis occurs until the solution inside a root hair and the soil are of the same _____.
skill: Describe the transport of water in plants
feedback: Osmosis occurs because of a difference in concentration. The solution inside a root hair becomes lower in concentration until water reaches the xylem vessels.

subject: Science
q: Why are plants called producers while animals are called consumers?
options: Plants produce oxygen, while animals consume oxygen.
Animals have the ability to move around and interact with each other. Plants cannot move.
Plants create their own water, while animals have to drink water to live.
Plants create their own food. Animals have to eat other substances to survive.
skill: Define photosynthesis and discuss its process
feedback: Through the process of photosynthesis, plants create their own food. The word create is similar in meaning to produce, so plants are known as producers. As animals cannot make their own food and have to eat other substances, they are known as consumers.

subject: Science
q: A healthy plant is taken from the garden and placed indoors. After some time, the plant dies. What is a likely reason for the plant's death?
options: The plant did not receive enough carbon dioxide.
The plant did not receive enough oxygen.
The plant did not receive enough sunlight.
The plant lost it roots after it was placed indoors.
skill: Define photosynthesis and discuss its process
feedback: When a plant is placed indoors, the amount of sunlight available to it is reduced. If the plant is placed in complete darkness, it will eventually die as sunlight is essential for photosynthesis. Without photosynthesis, the plant cannot create its own food.

subject: Science
q:
Which of the following statements show that photosynthesis is essential for humans?

I. Photosynthesis provides oxygen that humans need to live.
II. Photosynthesis allows plants to grow, and plants are a source of energy for humans.
III. Photosynthesis provides carbon dioxide that humans need to live.
skill: Define photosynthesis and discuss its process
feedback: Oxygen is a product of photosynthesis, and this gas is essential for humans. Plants are also a source of energy for all life on Earth as other living things, either directly or indirectly, feed on plants. Therefore, only I and II are correct.

subject: Science
q: The carbon dioxide needed for photosynthesis enters leaves through the
skill: Define photosynthesis and discuss its process
feedback: Carbon dioxide enters leaves through small openings known as stomata. These are located mainly on the underside of a leaf. The size of the stomata varies depending on the needs of the plant.

subject: Science
q: Which statement about photosynthesis is NOT true?
options: Carbon dioxide is a product of photosynthesis.
Glucose is produced as a result of photosynthesis.
Photosynthesis is essential for all life on Earth.
skill: Define photosynthesis and discuss its process
feedback: Carbon dioxide is NOT a product of photosynthesis. It is an input or reactant. Carbon dioxide combines with water in the presence of sunlight to produce glucose and oxygen.

subject: Science
q: Which statement about photosynthesis is NOT true?
options: Photosynthesis takes place continuously in leaves.
Carbon dioxide is essential for photosynthesis.
Photosynthesis takes place in the chloroplasts in leaves.
skill: Define photosynthesis and discuss its process
feedback: Photosynthesis takes place only during daytime in the presence of sunlight. It does not take place at night. Therefore, it is incorrect to say that photosynthesis takes place continuously in leaves.

subject: Science
q:
The equation below shows the reaction for photosynthesis. Which product is missing?

Carbon dioxide + Water → _____ + Oxygen
skill: Define photosynthesis and discuss its process
feedback: In photosynthesis, carbon dioxide and water combine to form glucose and oxygen. The glucose is then broken down to provide the energy needed for the plant's activities.

subject: Science
q: A cell is taken from the upper surface of a leaf. It has numerous chloroplasts. It is likely to be
skill: Describe the internal structure of a leaf, and its role in photosynthesis - Science
feedback: Cells near the upper surface of a leaf are either epidermal cells or palisade mesophyll cells. Of these two cell types, palisade mesophyll cells contain chloroplasts. Spongy mesophyll cells also have chloroplasts but they are not near the upper surface.

subject: Science
q: Which statement about leaves is NOT true?
options: All leaves are adapted to support photosynthesis.
All leaves are broad and flat.
All leaves have empty spaces between cells.
skill: Describe the internal structure of a leaf, and its role in photosynthesis - Science
feedback: Several, but NOT all, leaves are broad and flat. Some water plants have leaves that are long and thin so they can reach the surface of the water. The specific shape of a plant species depends on the environment it grows in.

subject: Science
q: When it rains, water droplets that fall on leaves
options: slide off the leaves and fall to the ground.
are taken to the roots by xylem vessels.
skill: Describe the internal structure of a leaf, and its role in photosynthesis - Science
feedback: Excessive water absorption can be harmful to leaves. Therefore, they are covered by a waterproof, waxy layer known as a cuticle. The cuticle prevents water drops from entering the leaf, so they slide off and fall to the ground.

subject: Science
q: Xylem vessels in plants carry water from _____ to leaves.
skill: Describe the internal structure of a leaf, and its role in photosynthesis - Science
feedback: Water is an essential input in photosynthesis. The roots of plants absorb water from the soil, and thin tube-like structures known as xylem vessels carry the water to the leaves.

subject: Science
q: Palisade mesophyll cells in leaves are tightly packed while spongy mesophyll cells are loosely packed. How does this support photosynthesis?
options: The tightly packed palisade mesophyll cells protect the spongy mesophyll cells below them from excessive sunlight.
Photosynthesis occurs at a higher rate in spongy mesophyll cells as they have more chloroplasts.
The tightly packed mesophyll cells near the upper surface ensure that no gases can escape the leaf.
Carbon dioxide and water vapour can move in the empty spaces between spongy mesophyll cells.
skill: Describe the internal structure of a leaf, and its role in photosynthesis - Science
feedback: Carbon dioxide and water vapour are essential inputs in the process of photosynthesis. Once they enter the leaf, they need to reach the chloroplasts where photosynthesis takes place. They reach these chloroplasts by moving in the empty spaces between spongy mesophyll cells.

subject: Science
q: Which cells do both plants and humans have?
skill: Describe the internal structure of a leaf, and its role in photosynthesis - Science
feedback: The surfaces of the bodies of both plants and humans are covered with epidermal cells. These cells protect the internal structure of the body.

subject: Science
q: The diagram above shows the internal structure of a leaf. Which labelled part does NOT have any chloroplasts?
skill: Describe the internal structure of a leaf, and its role in photosynthesis - Science
feedback: The part labelled as A is an epidermal cell. Epidermal cells protect the layers below. They do not have a role in photosynthesis and do not contain any chloroplasts.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following statements about stomata in leaves is NOT true?
options: The size of stomata is controlled by the movement of guard cells.
Stomata allow for the exchange of gases like carbon dioxide and oxygen.
The upper and lower surfaces of a leaf have an equal number of stomata.
The size of the stomata changes depending on the needs of the leaf.
skill: Describe the internal structure of a leaf, and its role in photosynthesis - Science
feedback: Stomata are openings that control the gases that enter and exit a leaf. The upper and lower surfaces of a leaf DO NOT usually have an equal number of stomata. The lower surface normally has more stomata.

subject: Science
q:
Humans depend on plants for

I. food.
II. oxygen.
III. energy.
IV. sunlight.
skill: Explain respiration in plants
feedback:
Plants are essential for the existence of human beings. They are consumed directly by humans for food, therefore providing energy. They also provide the oxygen humans use for respiration.

Plants depend on sunlight to make energy for themselves. However, humans do not depend on plants for sunlight.

subject: Science
q:
The equation below shows the reaction for respiration. Which product is missing?

Glucose + Oxygen → Energy + Carbon dioxide + _____
skill: Explain respiration in plants
feedback: In respiration, glucose is broken down by oxygen to release energy. Along with carbon dioxide, water is also a by-product. These by-products are used by plants as the reactants in photosynthesis.

subject: Science
q:
Which of the following processes provide the carbon dioxide that plants need for photosynthesis?

I. respiration
II. formation of glucose
III. gaseous exchange through stomata
skill: Explain respiration in plants
feedback: Carbon dioxide is one of the by-products of respiration. Carbon dioxide is also absorbed by plants during gaseous exchange through stomata.

subject: Science
q: Choose a difference between photosynthesis and respiration.
options: Water only occurs in the chemical equation for photosynthesis.
Photosynthesis can only occur in the afternoon while respiration occurs all the time.
Glucose is formed in photosynthesis and broken down in respiration.
Carbon dioxide is produced during photosynthesis and used in respiration.
skill: Explain respiration in plants
feedback: In photosynthesis, carbon dioxide and water react to create, or form, glucose. In respiration, this glucose is broken down in a reaction with oxygen. Therefore, the correct difference is: Glucose is formed in photosynthesis and broken down in respiration.

subject: Science
q: Choose a similarity between photosynthesis and respiration.
options: Chlorophyll is needed in both reactions.
Water is in the chemical equations of both reactions.
Both reactions can take place at night.
Oxygen is an input in both reactions.
skill: Explain respiration in plants
feedback: Water is an input, or reactant, in photosynthesis and a product in respiration. Therefore, water is in the chemical equations of both reactions.

subject: Science
q:
Arrange the steps showing the delivery of energy to a plant's roots in the correct order.

I. The sunlight absorbed is used to produce sugar.
II. Phloem vessels transport sugar from the leaves to the roots.
III. Sugar is broken down to release energy.
IV. Xylem vessels transport water from the roots to leaves.
skill: Explain respiration in plants
feedback:
The steps in the correct order are shown below:

IV. Xylem vessels transport water from the roots to leaves.
I. The sunlight absorbed is used to produce sugar.
II. Phloem vessels transport sugar from the leaves to the roots.
III. Sugar is broken down to release energy.

Photosynthesis can only occur when sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide are present.

subject: Science
q: Which statement about the relationship of photosynthesis and respiration is NOT true?
options: If respiration is stopped, photosynthesis will also stop after some time.
If photosynthesis is stopped, respiration will also stop after some time.
Respiration can only take place when photosynthesis is taking place.
Photosynthesis can use the carbon dioxide produced during respiration.
skill: Explain respiration in plants
feedback: Photosynthesis can only take place during daytime, while respiration takes place all the time as it to produce energy. Therefore, respiration does not only take place when photosynthesis is taking place.

subject: Science
q: When is the rate of photosynthesis in a plant equal to its rate of respiration?
options: late in the evening when photosynthesis is about to stop
when there is no net exchange of gases with the environment
when the rate of oxygen production is greater than carbon dioxide production
when the rate of carbon dioxide production is greater than oxygen production
skill: Explain respiration in plants
feedback: The by-products of respiration, carbon dioxide and water, are the reactants of photosynthesis. When the rates of the two reactions are equal, the exchange of gases occurs in a closed loop and no gases are exchanged with the environment.

subject: Science
q: What happens during cellular respiration and photosynthesis in plants?
options: Light energy is converted into chemical energy during photosynthesis whereas, chemical energy is converted into heat energy during respiration.
Chemical energy is converted into heat energy during photosynthesis whereas, light energy is converted into chemical energy during respiration.
Chemical energy is converted into heat energy during photosynthesis and respiration.
Light energy is converted into chemical energy during photosynthesis and respiration.
skill: Compare photosynthesis and respiration in plants
feedback: During photosynthesis, light energy is converted into chemical energy. During respiration, the chemical energy is converted into heat energy. Plants utilise sunlight to produce organic molecules such as glucose from simple molecules such as carbon dioxide and water. On the other hand, both plants and animals break down organic molecules such as glucose to produce energy through the process of respiration.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following statements is FALSE?
options: Respiration takes place in only green plants.
Respiration takes place in all plants.
Photosynthesis takes place only in green plants.
skill: Compare photosynthesis and respiration in plants
feedback: The statement that respiration takes place in only green plants is FALSE. Respiration occurs in all living organisms, animals and plants included. Through the process of respiration, the energy necessary to carry out life processes is generated.

subject: Science
q: At night, the concentration of carbon dioxide in the air in a forest is high. What is the main reason for this?
options: There is increased respiration by animals that are active at night.
Plants are unable to absorb carbon dioxide because their stomata are shut.
Plants begin to respire in the dark.
Plants can only use carbon dioxide for photosynthesis in the light.
skill: Compare photosynthesis and respiration in plants
feedback: The concentration of carbon dioxide is high during nighttime in a forest because plants only use carbon dioxide for photosynthesis in sunlight. At night, plants don't photosynthesise and instead only carry out respiration. As a result, plants release carbon dioxide into the atmosphere which is why its concentration increases.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following statements is correct?
options: ATP is needed in photosynthesis but not in respiration.
ATP is needed in respiration but not in photosynthesis.
ATP is needed in both photosynthesis and respiration.
ATP is not needed in either photosynthesis or respiration.
skill: Compare photosynthesis and respiration in plants
feedback: ATP is needed in both photosynthesis and respiration. All the steps of photosynthesis or respiration that require the expenditure of energy utilise molecules of ATP.

subject: Science
q: Which of the given information is correct for photosynthesis and respiration?
skill: Compare photosynthesis and respiration in plants
feedback: Option B is correct. Photosynthesis is an anabolic process whereas, respiration is a catabolic process. Photosynthesis uses light energy to synthesise glucose molecules from carbon dioxide. On the other hand, respiration uses oxygen to break down glucose into carbon dioxide to produce energy.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following conditions are necessary for photosynthesis but not for respiration?
skill: Compare photosynthesis and respiration in plants
feedback: Both sunlight and water, option A, are necessary for photosynthesis. Sunlight is not required for respiration to occur. However, glucose is required for respiration to occur.

subject: Science
q:
Which of the following statements are correct?

I.   Photosynthesis takes place during the day.
II.  Respiration takes place during the day as well as at night.
III. Photosynthesis takes place during the night.
IV. Respiration takes place during the night.
skill: Compare photosynthesis and respiration in plants
feedback: Statements I and II are correct. The process of photosynthesis occurs during daytime only whereas, the process of respiration occurs during the day as well as at night.

subject: Science
q: The diagram below shows the human gas exchange system. Which labelled part is responsible for the exchange of gases?
skill: Differentiate between breathing and respiration
feedback: The primary organs of the respiratory system are lungs, which carry out an exchange of gases as we breathe. Using the respiratory system, blood cells gather oxygen in the lungs and take it to the heart. The heart distributes this oxygen rich blood to the rest of the body.

subject: Science
q: When we breathe, _____ is inhaled, and _____ is exhaled.
options: oxygen and carbon dioxide; carbon dioxide
skill: Differentiate between breathing and respiration
feedback: When we breathe in, all the gases present in the air enters our lungs. However, when we exhale these gases including carbon dioxide leave, except for oxygen.

subject: Science
q: The ___ is a spongy like structure situated within the ribcage.
skill: Differentiate between breathing and respiration
feedback: The lungs are a pair of spongy, air-filled organs located on either side of the chest within the rib cage.

subject: Science
q: Which chemical reaction shows the CORRECT representation of respiration?
skill: Differentiate between breathing and respiration
feedback:
Respiration is the chemical process through which oxygen is taken into the body, and then transported to the cells through blood. Oxygen is then used in the reaction that releases energy from food, and in the production of carbon dioxide.

This process is shown by the chemical reaction below:
food \[+\] oxygen \[\rightarrow\] carbon dioxide \[+\] energy

subject: Science
q: During breathing,___ is the taking in of air and ___ is the taking out of air.
skill: Differentiate between breathing and respiration
feedback: The act of drawing air into the trachea and lungs is called inhalation. Exhalation is the act of taking air out of human body.

subject: Science
q: The air that we breathe out is different from the air we breathe in because
skill: Differentiate between breathing and respiration
feedback: When we exhale, we breathe out less oxygen but more carbon dioxide than when we inhale. The carbon that we breathe out as carbon dioxide comes from the carbon in the food we eat. Carbon dioxide is dissolved in the blood, carried to the lungs through circulation, and then breathed out.

subject: Science
q: Oxygen from the lungs to the cells, and carbon dioxide from the cells to lungs is transported through ___.
skill: Differentiate between breathing and respiration
feedback:
Blood transports gases, namely oxygen and carbon dioxide, between the lungs and rest of the body.
Oxygen enters the blood stream through the lungs. This oxygen rich blood then goes to the heart, which pumps it to all parts of our body. Our cells use oxygen to produce energy and carbon dioxide. Blood vessels take carbon dioxide from all parts of the body to the lungs. It is then exhaled out of the body because too much carbon dioxide is harmful to the body.

subject: Science
q: Respiration is the process through which ___ is broken down in our bodies to release energy.
skill: Differentiate between breathing and respiration
feedback: Respiration is the chemical process throuch which the cells of an organism obtain energy by breaking down food in the presence of oxygen. This results in the release of carbon dioxide and energy.

subject: Science
q: What is NOT a feature of alveoli?
skill: Describe the respiratory system - Biology
feedback: Alveoli are tiny air sacs within our lungs surrounded by blood capillaries. The exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide occur between alveoli and capillaries. There are no muscles in an alveoli.

subject: Science
q: Which structure is lined with tiny hair like structures?
skill: Describe the respiratory system - Biology
feedback: Tiny hair like structures called cilia are found in the respiratory tract to keep the airways clear of mucus and dirt. This  allows us to breathe easily and without irritation. Most of the respiratory tract from nose to bronchi, including <b>trachea</b,> is covered by cilia.

subject: Science
q:
The diagram shows the human respiratory system.


What are the labelled parts?
options: A is alveolus, B is trachea, C is bronchiole, D is bronchus
A is bronchus, B is trachea, C is bronchiole, D is alveolus
A is trachea, B is alveolus, C is bronchiole, D is bronchus
A is trachea, B is bronchus, C is bronchiole, D is alveolus
skill: Describe the respiratory system - Biology
feedback: The human respiratory system consists of the nose, larynx, trachea labelled A, bronchi (singular: bronchus labelled B), lungs, bronchioles labelled C, alveoli (singular: alveolus labelled D).

subject: Science
q:
Diagram shows front view of person's chest.


Label the structure.
skill: Describe the respiratory system - Biology
feedback: The lungs are protected by ribs. When we breathe in, the ribs move upward and out, and downwards and in when we breathe out.

subject: Science
q: What is the correct pathway for the movement of oxygen from the air to the blood inside the human body?
options: nose/mouth, larynx, bronchus, bronchioles, trachea, alveolus
nose/mouth, larynx, trachea, bronchioles, bronchi, alveolus
nose/mouth, larynx, trachea, bronchi, bronchioles, alveolus
nose/mouth, larynx, alveolus, trachea, bronchi, bronchioles
skill: Describe the respiratory system - Biology
feedback: Oxygen from the atmosphere enters the nose or the mouth when we breathe in air. Then it passes through the larynx, into the trachea. The trachea leads to the branches of the bronchi, leading to the lungs. In the lungs, the oxygen goes through smaller branches called bronchioles. These finally lead to the alveoli (singular: alveolus) that are single cell air sacs surrounded by blood capillaries. From here, oxygen enters our bloodstream.

subject: Science
q: _____ in the nose, purify the air that we breath in.
skill: Describe the respiratory system - Biology
feedback: Cilia are found in the respiratory tract that keep the airways clear from mucus and dirt, allowing us to breathe easily and without irritation. Most of the respiratory tract from the nose to the bronchi (including the trachea) is covered with cilia.

subject: Science
q: The main respiratory organ found inside the chest cavity is/are ______.
skill: Describe the respiratory system - Biology
feedback: The lungs are the primary organs of the respiratory system in humans. These are a pair of spongy, air-filled spaces located on either side of the chest. The trachea carries inhaled air into the lungs through its bronchi. The bronchi then divides into smaller and smaller branches, the bronchioles, finally becoming microscopic.

subject: Science
q: The muscles of the atria and ventricles _____ to force blood through the body.
skill: Trace the major pathway of blood through the circulatory system
feedback:
The muscles of the atria contract to force blood out of the heart.
They expand to let blood into the heart. The ventricles have thicker walls than the atria.

subject: Science
q: Veins transport blood from the body back to the right side of the heart. This blood contains _____.
skill: Trace the major pathway of blood through the circulatory system
feedback: Blood in the right side of the heart returns from the body after providing oxygen and nutrients to all parts of the body. Therefore, it contains very little oxygen. It is rich in carbon dioxide gained from the actively respiring cells.

subject: Science
q: The deoxygenated blood must be pumped to the _____ before it returns to the heart.
skill: Trace the major pathway of blood through the circulatory system
feedback: The primary role of the lungs is to provide oxygen to the blood. After delivering oxygen to all parts of the body, blood goes back to the heart through the veins. It is pumped to the lungs where it is oxygenated and then pumped to all parts of the body again.

subject: Science
q: The heart pushes blood around the body through tubes called _____.
skill: Trace the major pathway of blood through the circulatory system
feedback: Blood vessels are small tubes that carry blood to and from the heart. The vessels taking blood to the heart are called veins, while the vessels carrying blood away from the heart are called arteries.

subject: Science
q: The throbbing movements of the blood flow is called _____.
skill: Describe the organs involved in the circulatory system
feedback: Pulse is the rhythmic throbbing of the arteries as blood is propelled through them. Typically, a pulse can be felt in the wrists or the neck.

subject: Science
q: In the circulatory system, the deoxygenated blood is pumped to the _____.
skill: Describe the organs involved in the circulatory system
feedback: The deoxygenated blood is sent to the lungs to for oxygenation and to get rid of carbon dioxide.

subject: Science
q: What is the circulatory system composed of?
options: The heart, blood, and blood vessels
The heart, the brain, and the lungs
The lungs, the blood, and the blood vessels
The brain, the heart, and the blood vessels
skill: Describe the organs involved in the circulatory system
feedback: The circulatory system is composed of the heart and blood vessels, including arteries, veins, and capillaries. Lungs are the organ of respiration and part of respiratory system and brain is part of nervous system.

subject: Science
q: Which chamber of the heart delivers blood to the rest of the body?
skill: Describe the structure and function of the heart
feedback: The heart receives oxygenated blood from the left atrium. Blood then goes into the left ventricle to be further transported to the rest of the body.

subject: Science
q: Blood vessels called _____ carry blood to the two atria.
skill: Describe the structure and function of the heart
feedback:
The heart has two types of blood vessels. Those that carry blood from the heart and those carry blood to the heart. The vessels that carry blood back to the heart are called veins.
Pulmonary vein brings the blood back from the lungs and the venae cavae bring the blood back from the rest of the body.

subject: Science
q: The atria are separated from each other by a muscular wall. The atria are connected to ventricles by _____.
skill: Describe the structure and function of the heart
feedback: The atria are two upper chambers of the heart. The atria contracts to force blood into the ventricles through a valve. The valves ensure one-directional flow when the ventricles contract to send blood to the lungs and the rest of the body.

subject: Science
q: Haemoglobin is a protein that allows _____ to carry oxygen.
skill: Explain how blood is transported all over the human body
feedback: Red blood cells are shaped like a biconcave disc. Haemoglobin is a protein that combines with oxygen and transport oxygen all over the body.

subject: Science
q: Veins have low blood pressure, so they need _____ to make sure the blood flows in only one direction. Arteries only need them at the point of exit from the heart.
skill: Explain how blood is transported all over the human body
feedback: There is much lower blood pressure in veins than in arteries, therefore veins require valves to keep the blood flowing in only one direction. Arteries, however, carry blood with high pressure and only require valves when leaving the heart to maintain one directional flow.

subject: Science
q: The blood in our arteries is bright red compared to the dull red of the blood in our veins. What is the reason for the change in colour?
options: Blood in veins contains carbon dioxide.
Blood in arteries contains carbon dioxide.
Blood in veins contains a lot of oxygen.
Blood in arteries contains lots of oxygen.
skill: Explain how blood is transported all over the human body
feedback: The bright red colour of blood in the arteries is due to a high concentration of oxygen. There is a much lower concentration of oxygen in the veins, therefore, this blood has a dull red colour.

subject: Science
q: A patient has problems healing his wounds; his cuts take very long to stop bleeding. Which of the following might he be suffering from?
skill: Describe what blood is, and the components that make up blood
feedback: The dengue fever virus reduces the number of platelets in the blood. Platelets are essential for clotting the blood to stop bleeding. Hence, he must be suffering from dengue fever. Anaemia is a deficiency of red blood cells or haemoglobin. Leukaemia is a cancer of the blood, and AIDS is a disease that decreases white blood cells in the blood.

subject: Science
q: The AIDS virus attacks _____ and the body('s) _____ .
options: white blood cells, immune system fails.
platelets, cannot form clots so it continues to bleed.
all blood cells, fails to make new blood cells.
skill: Describe what blood is, and the components that make up blood
feedback: The AIDS virus attacks white blood cells. White blood cells are essential for the body's immune system. As a result, AIDS causes the body's immune system to fail.

subject: Science
q: Our bodies lose water in the processes of _____ in the form of vapours and sweat.
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback: Our bodies lose water during respiration in the form of water vapour and during excretion in the form of sweat and urine.

subject: Science
q:
What are possible consequences of obesity?
I.   heart diseases
II.  high blood pressure
III. diabetes
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback: Obesity, or continuous overeating, results in diseases like heart attack, blood pressure, and diabetes.

subject: Science
q: Why do we need to keep our bodies hydrated?
options: Drinking water keeps us full for longer and thus we do not gain excess weight.
The human body needs water to perform most of its important functions.
Drinking water helps a person gain weight if he/she is underweight.
Drinking water is important so that our bodies can retain excess fluid.
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback:
We should drink plenty of water and keep our bodies hydrated, because water helps our bodies perform most of the important functions, like circulation, digestion, and in absorption and transportation of nutrients.

We cannot lose weight or gain weight just by drinking water. To lose or gain weight, we need to watch our food intake and pair it with some exercises.

subject: Science
q:
Which of the following are advantages of exercising?

I.   increase in physical strength
II.  weight control
III. better mental health
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback: There are numerous advantages of exercising. Exercise increases our physical strength and helps in weight control. It also improves mental health by reducing anxiety and depression and by improving self-esteem.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following statements about mental health and is TRUE?
options: It doesn't really matter whether one sleeps for four hours or eight hours.
There is no relationship between sleep and weight gain.
Sleep is closely related to mental health.
Everyone can function well with 3 hours of sleep.
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback:
Sleep and mental health are closely connected.

Sleep affects our psychological state and mental health. Those with mental health problems are more likely to have sleep disorders.

subject: Science
q:
Which of the following are ways to remain healthy?

I.   Get enough sleep.
II.  Keep oneself hydrated.
III. Exercise regularly.
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback:
Getting enough sleep, keeping our bodies hydrated, and exercising regularly are important to live a healthy life.

Getting enough sleep is as important to our bodies as eating, drinking or breathing. It helps in reducing stress, improving mood, and remaining focused. It also boosts our immune system.

subject: Science
q:
Abdullah goes to a gym every day and exercises for almost two hours to keep himself fit and healthy. He also eats beans every day. Despite the hard work, he doesn't feel active. Which of the following statements about Abdullah's condition is/are TRUE?

I.   He should exercise more to stay active.
II.  Eating the same food everyday may have resulted in a deficiency of nutrients in his body.
III. He is overworking at the gym.
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback:
Excess of everything can be bad, be it exercise.

Overexercising can negatively impact our bodies. It can lead to stress, hormone imbalance, and exhaustion.

Eating the same food types everyday results in a deficiency of nutrients in one's body. If we continue to consume the same food every day, our bodies will lack several essential nutrients that are not part of that food source.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following statements is FALSE about physical health?
options: Physically fit people are less likely to fall ill because they have a strong immune system.
There is no relationship between physical and mental health.
Eating the same food type every day may result in lack of essential nutrients in our body.
Exercise is important to stay physically fit and healthy.
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback:
The statement, there is no relationship between physical and mental health is FALSE. A sound body supports a sound mind.

Physical and mental health are interconnected. Poor physical health can lead to mental health problems. Similarly, poor mental health can negatively affect the physical health.

subject: Science
q:
Which of the following can be harmful for our health?

I.   consuming alcohol
II.  not drinking water
III. playing mobile games occasionally
IV. eating fries everyday
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback:
Consuming alcohol, not drinking enough water, and eating fries everyday can be harmful for our health.
Fries contain high amounts of fats. A balanced diet does not consist of a large amount of fats.

Playing games on mobile phones occasionally doesn't affect our health.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following is a good way to remain active and healthy?
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback:
Walking everyday is an easy and good way to remain active and healthy.

Watching TV or playing videogames is fun, but they don't contribute to our physical health.

Similarly, as per the famous proverb, all work and no play makes Jack a dull boy, continuous studying without rest or relaxation can be harmful for our mental and physical health.

subject: Science
q: _____ shows the different food groups and how much of each food group creates a healthy diet.
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback: A food pyramid is a chart that shows that how many servings of a food group should be consumed in a healthy and balanced diet. The food at the bottom should be consumed more than the food at the top as indicated by the sizes of their sections.

subject: Science
q: What would happen if a person doesn't have a balanced diet?
options: There will be no effect on his/her physical or mental health.
His/her eyesight will become weak within a week.
He/she will have a strong immune system due to lack of nutrients.
He/she will be more likely to suffer from a physical disorder or malnutrition.
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback:
It is necessary to eat a variety of foods in the right proportion. A balanced diet supplies the nutrients that our bodies need to function effectively.

If a person doesn't consume a balanced diet, he/she is more likely to suffer from a physical disorder due to a lack of nutrients.

subject: Science
q: Why should we exercise?
options: to make ourselves hungry all the time
to spend less time on studies
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback: We should exercise to stay fit and make our bodies strong. Exercise increases stamina and endurance, both of which make our bodies strong.

subject: Science
q: It is important to consume a balanced diet so that
options: we are able to eat everything when we go on an expedition.
our bodies are prone to diseases, infections, and fatigue.
we are not scolded by our parents on not eating vegetables.
we get all the necessary nutrients for our body to function properly.
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback: It is important to consume a balanced diet so that we get all the necessary nutrients for our body to function properly. Our bodies need a variety of minerals and vitamins to function and this variety can be attained by having a balanced diet.

subject: Science
q: Why is it important to stay active?
options: to always stand first in class
to be able to fight with neighbours
to improve health and reduce the risk of falling ill
to be able to stay awake for more than 24 hours
skill: Identify ways to keep fit and stay healthy
feedback: We all need to stay fit and healthy because a healthy person is less likely to fall ill compared to an unhealthy fellow. Staying active is a very important way to generally stay in good health.

subject: Science
q: What is the charge of the part of the atom marked X?
options: can be either positive or negative
skill: Describe the structure of an atom
feedback: X is the nucleus of an atom containing positively charged protons and neutrons that are neutral. Therefore, the overall charge of a nucleus is positive.

subject: Science
q: Count the number of protons, neutrons, and electrons in the image below:
skill: Describe the structure of an atom
feedback:
Particles in the nucleus with a positive charge are protons. Protons = 3
Particles in the nucleus without charge are neutrons. Neutrons = 4
Particles going around the nucleus in shells with a negative charge are electrons. Electrons = 3

subject: Science
q:
In what ways are sodium atoms different from oxygen atoms?
I.   They have a different number of electrons.
II.  They have a different number of neutrons.
III. They have a different number of protons.
IV. They have different types of protons.
skill: Describe the structure of an atom
feedback:
Statements I, II and III are correct because sodium and oxygen atoms have different number of protons, neutrons, and electrons.
Sodium atom has 11p, 11e, and 12n.
Oxygen atom has 8p, 8e, and 8n.

subject: Science
q: An atom of sodium has 11 protons, 11 electrons, and 12 neutrons. What is the overall charge of the atom?
skill: Describe the structure of an atom
feedback:
The overall charge of an atom is always zero because the negative charges of the electrons cancel out the positive charges of the protons.
11 protons = +11
11 electrons = -11
Overall charge = +11 -11 = 0

subject: Science
q: A neutral carbon atom has 6 neutrons and 6 protons. How many electrons does it have?
skill: Describe the structure of an atom
feedback: Since the atoms are neutral, we know that the number of electrons must be equal to the number of protons. Therefore, the carbon atom has 6 electrons.

subject: Science
q: The atom overall is a neutral particle because _____
options: it does not have charged subatomic particles.
it has the same number of protons and neutrons.
it has the same number of protons and electrons.
it has the same number of neutrons and electrons.
skill: Describe the structure of an atom
feedback: The atom is neutral because it has an equal number of positively-charged protons and negatively-charged electrons.

subject: Science
q: Study the flowchart below and choose what X represents:
skill: Describe the structure of an atom
feedback: Protons, electrons and neutrons are subatomic particles.

subject: Science
q: What is the mass of an electron in Atomic Mass Unit (AMU)?
skill: Describe the structure of an atom
feedback: An electron is about 1836 times lighter than a proton or neutron. The mass of both proton and neutron is 1 AMU, therefore, the relative mass of an electron becomes \[\frac{1}{1836}\] AMU.

subject: Science
q: An element has 74 protons and 109 neutrons. What is its atomic number and mass number?
skill: Differentiate between atomic number and mass number
feedback:
Atomic number is the number of protons i.e. 74.
Mass number is the total number of protons and neutrons i.e. 74 + 109 = 183

subject: Science
q: If an atom has 7 protons, 7 neutrons, and 7 electrons. What will be its mass number?
skill: Differentiate between atomic number and mass number
feedback:
Mass number is the total number of protons and neutrons present in the nucleus of an atom.
7 protons + 7 neutrons = 14
Mass number = 14

subject: Science
q: What is the mass number of the atom shown below?
skill: Differentiate between atomic number and mass number
feedback:
Mass number is the total number of protons and neutrons present in the nucleus of an atom.
Blue circles in the centre with "+" sign are protons and green circles without charge are neutrons.
Counting all circles (protons + neutrons) in the nucleus gives the mass number of the atom.

subject: Science
q: Mass number of the sodium (Na) element is 23 and the number of neutrons is 12. How many electrons are in a sodium atom?
skill: Differentiate between atomic number and mass number
feedback:
No. of protons (in an atom) = no. of electrons
Mass number = no. of protons + no. of neutrons
No. of protons = mass number - no. of neutrons
= 23 - 12 = 11
= no. of electrons = 11

subject: Science
q: Calculate the number of neutrons of the following potassium (K) element.
skill: Differentiate between atomic number and mass number
feedback:
Mass number = 39
Atomic number = 19

Number of neutrons = mass number - atomic number
= 39 - 19
= 20

subject: Science
q: An atom of chlorine has 17 protons, 17 electrons, and 18 neutrons. What is the mass number of the chlorine atom?
skill: Differentiate between atomic number and mass number
feedback:
Mass number of an atom is the total number of its protons and neutrons.
Mass number = atomic number + neutron number
= 17 protons + 18 neutrons
= 35

subject: Science
q: Au is the element symbol for gold. What is the mass number of gold?
skill: Differentiate between atomic number and mass number
feedback:
The number on top of the element symbol is the mass number.
The number at the bottom is the atomic number of the element.

subject: Science
q: An element X has a mass number of 40 and neutron number of 22. How many electrons does it have altogether in its first two shells?
skill: Draw diagrams of the atomic structure of the first eighteen elements in the periodic table
feedback:
To find the number of electrons in element X, we need to know the atomic number.
atomic number = mass number - number of neutrons
= 40 - 22 = 18 electrons

The first shell will have 2 electrons and the second will have 8.
2 + 8 = 10 electrons in the first two shells.

subject: Science
q: Study the element symbol below and pick the correct electronic configuration of fluorine's L shell.
skill: Draw diagrams of the atomic structure of the first eighteen elements in the periodic table
feedback:
The symbol shows fluorine's atomic number at the bottom left.
The total number of electrons in the F atom = 9
The image with 2 electrons shows fluorine's K shell. The image with 7 electrons shows fluorine's L shell as highlighted below.

subject: Science
q: Silicon has an atomic number of 14. How many electrons are present in its K shell?
skill: Draw diagrams of the atomic structure of the first eighteen elements in the periodic table
feedback:
The K shell is the first shell in an electronic configuration and it contains a maximum of 2 electrons.

Therefore, silicon has 2 electrons in its K shell, 8 in its L shell and the remaining 4 in its M shell.

It is important to note that all elements, except hydrogen, have 2 electrons in their K shell.

subject: Science
q: The atomic number of boron is 5. The number of electrons in its L shell are ____.
skill: Draw diagrams of the atomic structure of the first eighteen elements in the periodic table
feedback:
The atomic number (no. of protons) = no. of electrons
The total number of electrons in boron = 5
The first shell or K-shell contains 2 electrons.
The second shell or L shell contains the remaining 3.

subject: Science
q: What is the correct electronic configuration of sulphur?
skill: Draw diagrams of the atomic structure of the first eighteen elements in the periodic table
feedback:
Sulphur has 16 electrons.
[2,8,7] and [2,7,8] are incorrect because they add up to 17 electrons.

The first (K) shell can hold 2 electrons. The second (L) shell can hold 8 electrons. And the remaining 6 electrons go to the third (M) shell.

Therefore, the electronic configuration of S is: [2,8,6].

subject: Science
q: What is the correct electronic configuration of sodium?
skill: Draw diagrams of the atomic structure of the first eighteen elements in the periodic table
feedback:
Sodium has 11 electrons.
[2,8] is incorrect because it adds up to only 10 electrons.

The first (K) shell can hold 2 electrons. The second (L) shell can hold 8 electrons. The last electron goes to the third (M) shell.

Therefore, the electronic configuration of sodium is: [2,8,1].

subject: Science
q: The diagram below is the electronic configuration of _____.
skill: Draw diagrams of the atomic structure of the first eighteen elements in the periodic table
feedback:
There are 13 electrons in the diagram and a neutral aluminium atom has 13 electrons.
The first shell contains a maximum of 2 electrons.
The second shell contains a maximum of 8 electrons.
The remaining 3 electrons of aluminium are accommodated in the third shell.
The electronic configuration of Al = [2,8,3].

subject: Science
q: Pick the correct electronic configuration of beryllium:
skill: Draw diagrams of the atomic structure of the first eighteen elements in the periodic table
feedback:
Beryllium has 4 electrons. The first shell can hold a maximum of 2 electrons and the second can hold a maximum of 8 electrons.
The first shell = 2 electrons.
The second shell = 2 electrons.

subject: Science
q: Which pair of elements are found in the same period in the periodic table?
skill: State the modern periodic law, and differentiate between a period and a group
feedback: Calcium and magnesium are found in period 4 of the periodic table. Elements in the same period have the same number of shells.

subject: Science
q: Which quantity is used to arrange elements in the periodic table?
skill: State the modern periodic law, and differentiate between a period and a group
feedback: The elements in the periodic table are arranged according to the number of protons in the nucleus, i.e., the atomic number.

subject: Science
q: In the following elements, which is most chemically reactive?
skill: State the modern periodic law, and differentiate between a period and a group
feedback: Reactivity increases down the group I, hence the metal which is more reactive than Caesium (Cs) is Francium (Fr).

subject: Science
q: The modern periodic law states that
options: If the elements are arranged in decreasing order of atomic numbers, their properties change unpredictably.
If the elements are arranged in increasing order of atomic numbers, their properties are not repeated in a periodic manner.
If the elements are arranged in increasing order of atomic numbers, their properties are repeated in a periodic manner.
If the elements are arranged in random order of atomic numbers, their properties change gradually in a periodic manner.
skill: State the modern periodic law, and differentiate between a period and a group
feedback: Periodic law states that if the elements are arranged in the order of their increasing atomic numbers, their properties are repeated in a periodic manner.

subject: Science
q: Platinum is in period _____ on the periodic table.
skill: State the modern periodic law, and differentiate between a period and a group
feedback: Platinum is a chemical element with the symbol Pt and atomic number 78. It is a dense, malleable, ductile, highly unreactive, precious, silverish-white transition metal. It is located in period 6 and group 10. Therefore, it is a transition element.

subject: Science
q: Aluminium is in period _____ on the periodic table.
skill: State the modern periodic law, and differentiate between a period and a group
feedback: Aluminium is the 13th element on the periodic table. It is located in period 3 and group 13.

subject: Science
q: Which group of elements belongs to the rare earth elements in the periodic table?
skill: State the modern periodic law, and differentiate between a period and a group
feedback: Scandium and yttrium are rare earth elements since they are found in the same ore deposits as the lanthanides and have similar chemical properties.

subject: Science
q: What is FALSE about nitrogen gas and carbon dioxide?
options: Elements in both substances are non-metals.
Both are made up of atoms.
skill: Differentiate between elements and compounds
feedback:
Nitrogen and carbon dioxide are gases present in the air. Nitrogen is a non-metal element found naturally in a gaseous state as N2 molecules.
Oxygen and carbon, which combine to make carbon dioxide, are also non-metals.

Everything in our universe is made up of atoms, including nitrogen and carbon dioxide.
Nitrogen gas is an element found naturally as N2, whereas carbon dioxide is a compound with one carbon and two oxygen atoms in one molecule.

subject: Science
q: Which of these materials contain only carbon in its element form?
skill: Differentiate between elements and compounds
feedback:
Carbon is a non-metal whereas wire and saucepans are made up of metals.
Carbon dioxide and methane are compounds made up of carbon and other elements.

Graphite and diamond are examples of materials that contain only the element carbon.
Graphite is used in lead pencils. Diamond is the world's hardest known material used in jewellery and for cutting glass.

subject: Science
q: What is TRUE about the copper used in wires?
options: Copper is naturally found in a gaseous state.
skill: Differentiate between elements and compounds
feedback: Copper (Cu) is a metallic element found naturally in a solid state. It is used in wires because it is cheap and a good conductor of electricity.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following statements about compounds is FALSE?
options: Compounds are made up of different elements.
There are two types of compounds: metal and non-metal.
Properties of compounds are different from the properties of the elements in them
skill: Differentiate between elements and compounds
feedback:
Recall that metals and non-metals are types of elements.
There are only around 100 elements known to man but the number of compounds is much larger.

subject: Science
q: What is the similarity between elements and compounds?
options: Both can be broken down into simpler form.
Both are composed of identical atoms.
Both are pure forms of matter.
skill: Differentiate between elements and compounds
feedback:
Elements and compounds are pure forms of matter just as all the atoms of an element are identical, so are the molecules in a compound.
Elements cannot be divided further into a simpler form but compounds can be divided into elements through chemical processes.
As a compound is made up of two or more different atoms, it is not represented by a symbol but by a chemical formula.

subject: Science
q: What is the symbol for the element aluminium?
skill: Differentiate between elements and compounds
feedback:
Aluminium is a metallic element. It is represented by the symbol Al, taken from first two letters of its name.
Au is the symbol for gold, Ag is for silver and Hg is the symbol for mercury.

subject: Science
q: What type of matter is shown in the following diagram?
skill: Differentiate between elements and compounds
feedback:
The given diagram shows molecules of nitrogen gas, which is an element.
Nitrogen gas atoms are found naturally in pair. Its molecule is represented by N2.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following is NOT a compound?
skill: Differentiate between elements and compounds
feedback:
Carbon dioxide (CO2), water (H2O) and sugar (C12H22O11) are made up of different elements in different ratios. Therefore, these are compounds.
Hydrogen (H), naturally found as an H2 molecule, is an element. All the atoms in a molecule of hydrogen are the same.

subject: Science
q: Chlorine has a valency of _____.
skill: Define valency
feedback:
Chlorine has 7 valence electrons. It will gain 1 electron to complete its outermost shell and gain a stable structure.
+1 is incorrect because it shows a loss of electrons whereas the chlorine atom must gain 1 electron.

subject: Science
q: What is the valency of atom X?
skill: Define valency
feedback:
X has 3 valence electrons. It will lose these 3 electrons to complete its outermost shell and gain a stable structure.
-5 is incorrect because gaining 5 electrons to complete the outermost shell is harder than losing 3 electrons.

subject: Science
q: Sulphur has an atomic number of 16. How many valence electrons does it have?
skill: Define valency
feedback:
Atomic number = number of protons.
Number of protons = number of electrons.
Sulphur has 16 electrons. It has 2 electrons in its first shell, 8 in the second shell, and the remaining 6 valence electrons in its valence or outermost shell.

subject: Science
q: The electronic configuration of neon is [2,8]. What is its valency?
skill: Define valency
feedback: The valency of noble gases like helium, neon, and argon is always 0. This is because they already have complete valence shells and have no more capacity to gain or lose electrons. Neon has 8 electrons in its outermost shell. Its shell is complete so its valency is 0.

subject: Science
q:
Which atoms have a valency of -1?

I.   hydrogen
II.  chlorine
III. sodium
skill: Define valency
feedback:
Both hydrogen and sodium have 1 electron in their outermost shell and a valency of +1.
Chlorine has 7 electrons in its outermost shell. It must gain 1 electron to complete its shell, hence, its valency is -1.

subject: Science
q:
Which of these atoms have 1 electron in their valence shell?

I.   lithium
II.  beryllium
III. sodium
skill: Define valency
feedback:
Both lithium and sodium have 1 electron in their outermost shell.
Beryllium has 2 electrons in its outermost shell so II is incorrect.

subject: Science
q: Oxygen has a valency of -2. How many valence electrons does it have?
skill: Define valency
feedback:
Valency of -2 means that oxygen must gain 2 electrons to complete 8 electrons in its outermost shell.
If oxygen needs 2 electrons to complete its valence shell, then it must have 6 valence electrons in its outermost shell already.

subject: Science
q: Choose the correct ion symbol and charge for the atomic structure given below.
skill: Formation of ions: cations and anions
feedback:
The atomic structure shows the following: p = 4, e = 2 and n = 5.
The atom with 4 protons is beryllium with the symbol Be.
4 protons meant that the neutral Be atom had 4 electrons and it lost 2 when it became an ion. The loss of 2 electrons results in a charge of +2.

subject: Science
q: If an ion has 24 protons and 27 electrons, what is the overall charge of the ion?
skill: Formation of ions: cations and anions
feedback:
Charges:

24 protons = +24
27 electrons = -27
Overall charge = +24 - 27 = -3

subject: Science

subject: Science
q:
Magnesium (Mg) belongs to group IIA and period 3 of the periodic table. Barium (Ba) belongs to group IIA and period 6. Identify the correct statement(s) about them.
  1. Ba has three more shells than Mg.
  2. Ba has three more valence electrons than Mg.
  3. Mg has a higher atomic number than Br.
skill: State the modern periodic law, and differentiate between a period and a group - Science
feedback:
I only. The periods in a periodic table represent the number of shells that electrons occupy in an atom and the groups represent the number of valence electrons. Both Ba and Mg have two valence electrons i.e. the number of electrons in their outermost shells. However, Ba has six shells, which means that the electrons that are present in Ba atom occupy a total of six shells, whereas all electrons in Mg atom occupy only 3 shells.

Atoms have an equal number of protons and electrons, and the atomic number represents the number of protons. Therefore, we can conclude that Ba has a higher atomic number than Mg since electrons in Ba atom occupy three more shells than Mg atom.

subject: Science
q: In a periodic table, elements of the same column or group have the same number of _____ and elements of the same row or period have the same number of _____.
skill: State the modern periodic law, and differentiate between a period and a group - Science
feedback: In a periodic table, elements of the same column have the same number of valence electrons and elements of the same row have the same number of shells. Valence electrons are the electrons that are in the outermost shell of an atom, also known as its valence shell. For example, Na and K are in group IA as both of them have one valence electron. However, Na has three shells and K has four shells. Therefore, Na is in period 3 and K is in period 4 of the periodic table.

subject: Science
q:
Fluorine (F) and bromine (Br) belong to group VIIA of the periodic table. However, F belongs to period II and Br belongs to period IV. Identify the correct statement(s) about them.
  1. F has two valence electrons and Br has four.
  2. Both F and Br have seven shells.
  3. Atomic number of Br is greater than that of F.
skill: State the modern periodic law, and differentiate between a period and a group - Science
feedback: III only. Since Br is in period IV and group VIIB, it has four shells and seven valence electrons. F has two shells and seven valence electrons. As Br has more shells than F, Br must as well have more electrons than F. In a neutral atom, the number of electrons is equal to the number of protons in its nucleus. Therefore, the atomic number of Br (which is the number of protons) is higher than that of F.

subject: Science
q:
Identify the correct statement(s) about elements that belong to the same period.

  1. They have the same number of valence electrons.
  2. They have the same chemical properties.
  3. They have the same physical properties.
options: None of the statements are correct.
skill: State the modern periodic law, and differentiate between a period and a group - Science
feedback:
None of the statements are correct. Elements of the same period have the same number of shells. For example, carbon (C) and oxygen (O) belong to period 2 because both of them have two shells. However, C has four valence electrons and O has six. Therefore, their chemical properties are different. The number of valence electrons determines how an element reacts (if it does) and whether it forms ions or not.

Physical properties of elements depend on their atomic masses i.e. the number of protons and neutrons. Since C and O have atomic masses of 12 and 16 amu, respectively, they have different weights, volumes, densities, melting points, and boiling points.

subject: Science
q:
Identify the correct statement(s) about elements in the same group.

  1. They have the same number of valence electrons, but different number of shells.
  2. They have the same chemical properties.
  3. They have the same physical properties.
skill: State the modern periodic law, and differentiate between a period and a group - Science
feedback:
The correct statements are I and II only.For example, F and Cl are in group VIIA because they have 7 valence electrons. However, F has two shells and Cl has three shells. Therefore, F is in period 2 and Cl is in period 3 of the periodic table.

Due to the same number of valence electrons, elements of the same group have very similar chemical properties. Elements react differently during chemical reactions due to the different number of valence electrons. For example, sodium (Na) is a highly reactive group I element because it has only one valence electron and it is a radical. It loses an electron to form a positive cation, \[\ce{Na+}\]. Cl, however, gains an electron to form a negative anion, \[\ce{Cl^{-}}\], because it has seven valence electrons.

However, elements in the same group have different physical properties because they have different atomic masses. Therefore, they have different weight, volume, density, melting, and boiling points.

Adding slowly is important because if we add too much in one go, it is possible that the dissolving limit may have already been crossed and not all of \[\ce{MgCO3}\] will dissolve. subject: Science
q: Sara has a clear solution of aqueous magnesium carbonate (\[\ce{MgCO3}\]). How can she find out what type of solution (unsaturated/saturated/supersaturated) it is?

topic: Saturated, unsaturated, supersaturated solutions and dilution of solution
skill: Differentiate between saturated, unsaturated, and supersaturated solutions, and describe dilution and concentration
feedback: Sara can find out the type of this solution by adding a crystal of\[\ce{MgCO3}\]. If \[\ce{MgCO3}\] dissolves, the solution was unsaturated since unsaturated solutions can dissolve more solute. If \[\ce{MgCO3}\] does not dissolve and settle at the bottom, it means the solution was saturated. Saturated solutions already contain maximum amount of dissolved solute. If \[\ce{MgCO3}\] does not dissolve and causes crystallisation, it means the solution was supersaturated. Supersaturated solutions contain more solute than their dissolving capacity, which is why the addition of any more causes rapid crystallisation.
