Therefore, the solution was already saturated and had reached its maximum solute dissolving capacity, which is why it could not dissolve any more.

subject: Science
q: Fog is produced when water vapours are mixed with smoke particles present in the air. It is a/an _____.
skill: Prepare saturated and unsaturated solutions
feedback: Smoke and water vapours form gas in liquid type of solutions. In fog, smoke is mixed with water vapour in excess quantity, which makes the solution saturated with smoke particles.

subject: Science
q: Which type of mixtures are formed when sugar and salt are dissolved in water?
skill: Prepare saturated and unsaturated solutions
feedback: When we dissolve sugar and salt in water, their particles are completely dissolved. None of the particles are seen in the solution. The solutions in which the solute particles are uniformly and completely dissolved in the solvent are called homogeneous solutions.

subject: Science
q: When making jelly, you accidentally use twice as much jelly powder as the recipe requires. You stir and stir but you cannot get all the jelly to dissolve. This is because the solution is_____.
skill: Prepare saturated and unsaturated solutions
feedback: When you accidentally use twice the amount of jelly powder that is required by a recipe, it means that you are trying to dissolve excess solute particles in the solvent. It makes the solution saturated.

subject: Science
q: You and your friend have a contest to see who can make iced tea the fastest. Which of the following would NOT help you or your friend win?
skill: Prepare saturated and unsaturated solutions
feedback:
Cooling water reduces the potential of water to dissolve the solute. This is one of the reasons that solubility of the solute increases with an increase in temperature.
Smaller crystals easily and quickly dissolve in water because of more surface area.
Heating water increases the solubility of the solute. Also, it is easier to make a saturated solution in hot water than in cold water.
Finally, the faster you stir, the sooner the solute will dissolve. You can try these tricks to win the contest.

subject: Science
q: 40 g of a solute is dissolved in 80 g of a solvent. The particle model shows the following image of this solution. What do you observe in this experiment?
skill: Distinguish between aqueous, dilute and concentrated solutions
feedback: The number of solute particles in the solvent is high according to the given image. Therefore, it can be concluded that it is a concentrated solution.

subject: Science
q: A student tries to dissolve a few pebbles in water. After some time, the pebbles settle down at the bottom of the beaker. What type of solution is this?
skill: Distinguish between aqueous, dilute and concentrated solutions
feedback: Pebbles can not dissolve in water, as shown in the image. Therefore, this is NOT a solution. Although the solvent is water, a solution is a mixture where all the solute particles are uniformly distributed in the solvent. Thus, it is NOT an aqueous solution either.

subject: Science
q: Solution 'A' has 30 g of solute dissolved in 80 g of water. If we add 30 g of water in the same solution, the new solution is called 'B'. What do you conclude from this experiment?
options: Solution A was a dilute solution.
Adding more solvent to the solution dilutes it.
Solution B is not a solution any more.
skill: Distinguish between aqueous, dilute and concentrated solutions
feedback: In the given experiment, solution A is a concentrated solution. Adding more water to it makes it a dilute solution. Therefore, solution B is a dilute solution with more solvent and less solute.

subject: Science
q: Heat flows from a hotter body to a _____ one.
skill: Describe the sources and effects of heat
feedback: When two objects of different temperature are placed together, energy will always transfer from the hotter to the colder object.  Heat is energy on the move.

subject: Science
q: Fuel that provides heat energy includes _____.
skill: Describe the sources and effects of heat
feedback: We use different kinds of fuel that provide heat energy. Wood, coal, petroleum, and natural gas are some of the major fuels.

subject: Science
q: When ice is heated, it _____ to form water.
skill: Describe the sources and effects of heat
feedback: Heat causes expansion of matter that is solid, liquid, and gases. When ice is heated, it expands to form water.

subject: Science
q: The melting of ice is an example of _____.
options: a change of state of matter
skill: Describe the sources and effects of heat
feedback: Heat changes states of matter. For example, heat can melt ice. When ice melts, it converts from a solid state to a liquid state.

subject: Science
q: Molten lava that erupts out of a volcano is an example of _____.
skill: Describe the sources and effects of heat
feedback: The natural heat trapped underground is called geothermal energy. Volcanoes, geysers, and hot springs allow the escape of hot substances from the inside of the Earth.

subject: Science
q: Which image is NOT showing the transfer of heat?
skill: Explain the flow of heat from a hot body to a colder body
feedback: In all the examples except the football on a table, heat is being transferred.

subject: Science
q: An iron ball at 40 degrees Celsius is dropped in a mug containing water at 40 degrees Celsius. The heat will
options: flow from iron ball to water.
flow from water to iron ball.
skill: Explain the flow of heat from a hot body to a colder body
feedback: Since both objects are at the same temperature, there will be no transfer of heat. Heat transfer occurs between objects when there is a temperature difference.

subject: Science
q: One litre of water at 30 degrees Celsius is mixed with one litre of water at 50 degrees Celsius. The temperature of the mixture will be
options: more than 50 degrees Celsius but less than 80 degrees Celsius.
between 30 degrees Celsius and 50 degrees Celsius.
skill: Explain the flow of heat from a hot body to a colder body
feedback: Heat is always moving. If two objects or substances are at different temperatures, heat will always move out of the warmer object or substance, and into the cooler object or substance. This heat transfer will continue until the objects are the same temperature. Hence, the final temperature is between 30 degrees Celsius and 50 degrees Celsius.

subject: Science
q:
Between which two objects will the transfer of heat be the slowest?
CasesObjects with different temperaturesObjects with different temperatures
IObject A, 80 degrees Celsius Object B, 20 degrees Celsius
IIObject C, 60 degrees CelsiusObject D, 40 degrees Celsius
IIIObject E, 40 degrees CelsiusObject F, zero degrees Celsius
IVObject G, 70 degrees CelsiusObject H, 20 degrees Celsius
skill: Explain the flow of heat from a hot body to a colder body
feedback:
Let's first find the temperature difference between objects in each case.

CasesObjects with different temperaturesObjects with different temperaturesTemperature difference
IObject A, 80 degrees Celsius Object B, 20 degrees Celsius60 degrees Celsius
IIObject C, 60 degrees CelsiusObject D, 40 degrees Celsius20 degrees Celsius
IIIObject E, 40 degrees CelsiusObject F, zero degrees Celsius40 degrees Celsius
IVObject G, 70 degrees CelsiusObject H, 20 degrees Celsius50 degrees Celsius

The temperature difference between objects C and D is 20 degrees Celsius, which is the lowest of all the other objects.
Hence, the heat transfer between objects C and D will be the slowest.

subject: Science
q:
Between which two objects will heat transfer the fastest?
CasesObjects with different temperaturesObjects with different temperatures
IObject A, 80 degrees Celsius Object B, 20 degrees Celsius
IIObject C, 60 degrees CelsiusObject D, 40 degrees Celsius
IIIObject E, 40 degrees CelsiusObject F, zero degrees Celsius
IVObject G, 70 degrees CelsiusObject H, 20 degrees Celsius
skill: Explain the flow of heat from a hot body to a colder body
feedback:
Let's first find the temperature difference between objects in each case.

CasesObjects with different temperaturesObjects with different temperaturesTemperature difference
IObject A, 80 degrees Celsius Object B, 20 degrees Celsius60 degrees Celsius
IIObject C, 60 degrees CelsiusObject D, 40 degrees Celsius20 degrees Celsius
IIIObject E, 40 degrees CelsiusObject F, zero degrees Celsius40 degrees Celsius
IVObject G, 70 degrees CelsiusObject H, 20 degrees Celsius50 degrees Celsius

Observe that the temperature difference between object A and B is 60 degrees Celsius, which is the highest difference among all the objects.
Hence, the heat transfer between object A and B will be the fastest.

subject: Science
q: Liquids and gases are poor conductors of heat because their molecules
options: do not collide with each other.
skill: Explain conduction through experiment, and recognise conduction and conducting materials in the environment
feedback: Solids are good conductors of heat while liquids & gases are bad conductors. Molecules of liquids and gases transfer heat by convection. It means their molecules carry the heat and move. They don't conduct. In solids the molecules are tightly packed and hence cannot move from place to place.

subject: Science
q: All the statements are true EXCEPT _____
options: The handles of cooking utensils are made up of wood or plastic.
Rubber is a good conductor of heat.
skill: Explain conduction through experiment, and recognise conduction and conducting materials in the environment
feedback: Rubber is a bad conductor of heat. Heat cannot move easily through rubber. Fur and feathers act as insulators and keep animal bodies warm. Woollen cloths have air pockets which reduce heat transfer, keeping us warm. The handles of cooking utensils are made from insulators like wood and plastic so that the heat does not harm our hands.

subject: Science
q: A silver spoon is dipped in a cup of ice cream. Its other end
options: becomes cold by the process of conduction.
becomes cold by the process of evaporation.
becomes cold by the process of radiation.
skill: Explain conduction through experiment, and recognise conduction and conducting materials in the environment
feedback: The end of a silver spoon becomes cold by the process of conduction. Heat transfers from a hot object to a cold object. Hence, heat will transfer from the silver spoon to the ice cream. The heat will transfer until both objects attain the same temperature.

subject: Science
q: Which of these is an insulator?
skill: Explain conduction through experiment, and recognise conduction and conducting materials in the environment
feedback: Wood is NOT a good conductor of heat. Iron, copper, and silver are conductors of heat. Wood is an insulator of heat.

subject: Science
q: Identify the object that slows down the transfer of heat.
skill: Explain conduction through experiment, and recognise conduction and conducting materials in the environment
feedback: Thermos bottles use air or a vacuum to slow the transfer of heat by conduction. Air is a bad conductor of heat. Irons, electric kettles, and cooking utensils are made up of metals that are good conductors of heat.

subject: Science
q: Which statement is TRUE about ocean currents?
options: Ocean currents occur because of the uneven temperatures of the water in the seas.
Ocean currents occur because of the even temperature of the water in the seas.
The sun heats the sea at the polar regions more than at the equatorial regions of the sea.
Water near the equator is cooler than  water near polar regions.
skill: Explain convection through experiment, and recognise convection in the environment
feedback: Ocean currents occur because of the uneven temperatures of the water in the seas. The water around the equator is much warmer than that in polar regions. This warm water flows along the surface from the equator to the poles, while colder, denser water from the poles flows below the surface of the ocean towards the equator. In this way, a convection current is created.

subject: Science
q: Ventilation in rooms is due to
skill: Explain convection through experiment, and recognise convection in the environment
feedback: Ventilation provides a continuous supply of fresh air for the people sitting in a room. To ventilate a room, windows and doors are left open. Air that is warm and light rises and passes through the ventilators at the top of a wall. Fresh air then enters through the doors and windows to take the place of the warm air. So, ventilation occurs due to convection.

subject: Science
q: The figure below shows a land breeze. Label the point X.
skill: Explain convection through experiment, and recognise convection in the environment
feedback: The air over land is cooler than the air over the sea. The air above the sea becomes warm, and then expands and rises. The cooler air from the land starts moving towards the sea. This is known as a land breeze. So, the missing information is warm air rises.

subject: Science
q: The picture shows:
skill: Explain convection through experiment, and recognise convection in the environment
feedback: The figure shows a sea breeze. During the day, the land heats up quickly by the heat of the sun. As a result, the air above it becomes hot. As this air expands and rises, the cooler air above the sea blows in to takes its place. This is called a sea breeze.

subject: Science
q: Compared to hot air, cool air is
skill: Explain convection through experiment, and recognise convection in the environment
feedback: In hot air, molecules vibrate vigorously and move apart. This causes air to expand and become less dense. However, the molecules of cold air are relatively close to each other in comparison to hot air. This means that, compared to hot air, cold air is dense.

subject: Science
q: Wind blows due to
skill: Explain convection through experiment, and recognise convection in the environment
feedback: When air heats up, it expands and becomes less dense. Warm air rises and cool air rushes in to fill the empty space. This movement of air is called wind. This happens due to convection.

subject: Science
q: Warm air rises because of
skill: Explain convection through experiment, and recognise convection in the environment
feedback: When air heats up, it expands and becomes less dense. Warm air rises and cool air rushes in to fill the empty space. This happens because of convection.

subject: Science
q: Which of the following is NOT an application of convection?
skill: Explain convection through experiment, and recognise convection in the environment
feedback:
The heating of a rod is an application of conduction. Conduction occurs due to the vibration of particles in the rod. When the rod is heated, heat energy moves from the hot end of the rod to the cool end.
The remaining examples/applications show the transfer of heat through convection.

subject: Science
q: Wooden bridges in the northern part of Pakistan are often painted black and white. In winters, the black parts feel warm and the white parts feel cold. This is because
options: white is a good absorber of heat while black is a good emitter of heat.
black is a good absorber and radiator of heat while white is a poor absorber of heat.
white is a good radiator of heat while black is a poor radiator of heat.
black is a poor radiator of heat but a good absorber of heat.
skill: Explain radiation through experiment, and recognise radiation in the environment
feedback: White and light colours are bad absorbers of heat. This keeps the white part of the bridge cool. Black is a good absorber as well as radiator of heat, hence it keeps the black sections of the bridge warm.

subject: Science
q: The walls and ceilings of houses are often painted white or in light colours
options: to enhance the beauty of the houses.
to increase the absorption of heat.
to reduce the absorption of heat.
to reduce the emission of heat.
skill: Explain radiation through experiment, and recognise radiation in the environment
feedback: The walls and ceilings of houses are painted white to reduce the absorption of heat. White and light colours are bad absorbers of heat. This keeps the houses cool in summers.

subject: Science
q:
Which of the following statements are TRUE for greenhouses?

I.   Radiation from the sun passes through the glass or plastic and warms up the soil and plants.
II.  Plants and soil absorb and emit radiation and increase the temperature in the greenhouse.
III. In cold areas, a greenhouse is used to magnify heat and sunlight for plants.
skill: Explain radiation through experiment, and recognise radiation in the environment
feedback: In cold areas, a greenhouse is used to magnify heat and sunlight for plants. Radiation from the sun passes through the glass or plastic and warms up the soil and plants. Plants and soil absorb and emit radiation and increase the temperature in the greenhouse. Plants grow well in the increased temperature of the greenhouse. Hence all statements are true.

subject: Science
q: Which tank should be used to store petrol?
options: A as it has a shiny, silvery, and smooth surface that will reflect heat, and will prevent the petrol from heating up.
A as it has a shiny, silvery, and smooth surface that will reflect less heat, preventing the petrol from heating up.
B as it has a dull and black surface that will radiate less heat, preventing petrol from heating up.
B as it has a dull and black surface that will radiate more heat and prevent the petrol from heating up.
skill: Explain radiation through experiment, and recognise radiation in the environment
feedback: Fuel storage tanks have shiny, silvery, and smooth surfaces that reflect heat and keep the petrol cool. A black and dull surface on a tank will absorb heat and heat up the petrol.

subject: Science
q: It is winter season in Murree. Asif and Ali are wearing different colour clothes. Identify who will feel colder.
options: Ali will feel colder as black is a good absorber of heat.
Asif will feel colder as white is a good absorber of heat.
Asif will feel colder as white is a poor absorber of heat.
Ali will feel cold as black is a poor absorber of heat.
skill: Explain radiation through experiment, and recognise radiation in the environment
feedback: White and light-coloured clothes are poor absorbers of heat, so people are advised to wear such clothes in the summer. Since black and dark-coloured clothes are good absorbers of heat, they are popular in winter. Hence, Ali will feel cold because he is wearing white clothes.

subject: Science
q: People should wear dark-coloured clothes in
skill: Explain radiation through experiment, and recognise radiation in the environment
feedback: We should wear dark-coloured clothes in winters because they absorb more heat from the sun and, hence, keep us warm.

subject: Science
q: What surfaces BEST emit radiation?
skill: Explain radiation through experiment, and recognise radiation in the environment
feedback: Black surfaces are good radiators of heat. White and light-coloured surfaces are poor emitters of radiation.

subject: Science
q: Mercury in a thermometer at room temperature will contract and fall if the _______.
options: temperature stays the same for too long
skill: Explain thermal expansion and contraction of solids, liquids and gases
feedback: Mercury in a thermometer is a liquid. All liquids expand on heating and contracts on heating. Likewise, mercury in a thermometer will contract and fall at a lower temperature.

subject: Science
q: Telephone line wires appear to sag, or dip downward, more in summers than in winters as the wires _____ between the poles.
skill: Explain thermal expansion and contraction of solids, liquids and gases
feedback: Telephone line wires appear to sag more in summers than in winters as the wires expand between the poles. The core of the wires is made from copper. Metals expand when heated and contract when cooled. The higher temperature in summer makes the copper expand and the lines get slightly longer.

subject: Science
q: Railway tracks have small gaps to prevent trains from falling off the track due to _____.
skill: Explain thermal expansion and contraction of solids, liquids and gases
feedback:
When railway tracks are laid, the engineers leave a small gap between two rails. All metals expand when heated. If two railway tracks are laid together without any gap, they will push against each other when they expand in the daytime because of the heat of the sun. They cool down in the night and will contract and return to their original state.

This constant pushing and pulling against one another when they expand and contract will result in the weakening of the joints between the two tracks. The two tracks may also break free from one another. The trains can derail, resulting in major accidents.

subject: Science
q: Ice _____ when heated to form water.
skill: Explain thermal expansion and contraction of solids, liquids and gases
feedback: When ice is heated, it contracts. The water molecules get closer together and the water occupies less volume. All liquids expand when cooled except water. Water is one of the few exceptions to this behaviour. When liquid water is cooled, it contracts until a temperature of approximately 4 degrees Celsius is reached. After that, it expands slightly until it reaches the freezing point, and then when it freezes, it expands by approximately 9 percent.

subject: Science
q: A pressure cooker explodes when you overheat it. This is an example of _____.
skill: Explain thermal expansion and contraction of solids, liquids and gases
feedback: When you heat a gas, its temperature and pressure both increase and cause a gas to expand. A pressure cooker is an example of an object that is affected by this expansion. Continuous heating will keep on increasing the expansion and results in the damage or explosion of the pressure cooker.

subject: Science
q: Another term for heat expansion is
skill: Explore the effects and applications of expansion and contraction of solids
feedback:
Another term for eat expansion is thermal expansion. The term refers to the expansion that occurs as a result of a change in temperature, not necessarily an increase in temperature.

Often, thermal expansion is a better term than heat expansion, since not all materials expand on heat. For example, ice expands as the temperature decreases. This is thermal expansion, but not heat expansion.

subject: Science
q:
We have three dice. One is made of bone, one is made of copper, and one is made of wood. We measure their volume, and find they have exactly the same volume.

Then, we leave the dice in the Sun for fifteen minutes, and we measure the volume again.

We find that all dice have increased in volume. But have their volumes increased at the same rate?
options: Yes. They have the same increase in volume because of heat expansion.
No. The wooden dice has increased the most in volume.
No. The bone dice has increased the most in volume.
No. The copper dice has increased the most in volume.
skill: Explore the effects and applications of expansion and contraction of solids
feedback: The copper dice is made of a metal, and metals are good conductors of heat. We can expect the copper dice to have heated up fastest, and expanded the most in volume.

subject: Science
q: Heat expansion is the result of an increase in the movement of
skill: Explore the effects and applications of expansion and contraction of solids
feedback: As the temperature goes up, the movement of molecules increases. As the molecules move or vibrate faster, they take up more space, which leads to an increase of volume. This increase of volume as a result of an increase of temperature is called heat expansion.

subject: Science
q: Most materials increase in volume when the temperature goes up. This is called heat expansion. But in which state do materials expand the fastest, and in which state do they expand the slowest?
options: Gases expand fastest. Liquids expand slowest.
Liquids expand fastest. Solids expand slowest.
Gases expand fastest. Solids expand slowest.
Solids expand fastest. Gases expand slowest.
skill: Explore the effects and applications of expansion and contraction of solids
feedback:
Expansion occurs when the temperature goes up, and molecules vibrate more, taking up more space. When molecules are further apart, they can move more easily and expand more easily.

This is the reason that gases expand fastest when temperature increases, and solids expand slowest in comparison with the same temperature increase.

subject: Science
q:
Most materials expand when the temperature increases.

What happens to ice, if the temperature rises from -4° C to 0° C?
options: The volume of the ice increases.
The volume of the ice decreases.
The volume of the ice remains the same.
skill: Explore the effects and applications of expansion and contraction of solids
feedback:
Most materials expand as the temperature increases, but not all. Water and ice are an exception. Ice expands as it gets colder. Water has its greatest density at 4° C.

This is the reason that icebergs can float on water.

subject: Science
q:
Which of the following factors affect the extent of volume expansion of a liquid?
I. original volume of the liquid
II. color of the liquid
III. rise in the temperature
IV. properties of the liquid
skill: Describe the uses of expansion and contraction of liquids
feedback:
Liquids have a definite volume; they show volume expansion only on heating. The extent of volume expansion of a liquid depends on the following three factors:

1. Original volume of the liquid
2. Rise in the temperature
3. Properties of liquid

subject: Science
q: Mercury expands about five times as much as an equal _____ of steel.
skill: Describe the uses of expansion and contraction of liquids
feedback: Liquids expand more than solids because particles in liquids are less tightly packed than in solids. Mercury expands about five times as much as an equal volume of steel.

subject: Science
q: When a liquid expands, it increases in _____.
skill: Describe the uses of expansion and contraction of liquids
feedback: If a liquid, such as water, expands as a result of a temperature increase, the volume increases, and the weight stays the same.

subject: Science
q: A car radiator has space for the coolant to _____.
skill: Describe the uses of expansion and contraction of liquids
feedback: When we add coolant in a car's radiator, it should not be filled completely. Heat generated by a car makes the coolant expand. So, if there is less space inside, then the coolant will jump out after the lid of the radiator is opened. Therefore, we are advised to keep our face far from the lid when opening a radiator.

subject: Science
q: Whenever _____ is stored in a closed container, space must be left for expansion to take place.
skill: Describe the uses of expansion and contraction of liquids
feedback: Whenever a liquid like oil is stored in a closed container, space must be left for expansion to take place as almost all liquids expand on heating.

subject: Science
q: Two astronauts try talking on the Moon but can't hear each other. They return to their spacecraft and now they can hear each other clearly. Why?
options: The moon does not allow the transfer of any type of energy in the form of waves.
Sound waves cannot travel in space or vacuum.
The air inside the spacecraft is a better conductor of sound waves than the air on the Moon.
skill: Explain waves and their types
feedback:
Sound waves need a material medium (metal, water or air) to travel and transfer energy. Sound waves cannot travel through a vacuum and the atmosphere of the Moon is a vacuum (no air, empty space). That is why astronauts can't hear each other on the Moon because there is no medium to transfer sound waves.

However, their spacecraft is filled with air and oxygen and sound waves can travel inside the spacecraft through vibrations in the air.

subject: Science
q: A longitudinal wave consists of
skill: Explain waves and their types
feedback:
Compressional wave is another name for a longitudinal wave. Longitudinal waves are made up of a series of compressions and rarefactions. But one compressional wave includes one compression and one rarefaction only.
The distance between two consecutive compressions is called a wavelength. Crests and troughs are parts of a transverse wave. And 2 compressions and 2 rarefactions make 2 compressional waves.

subject: Science
q: A and B are points in a wave. What are A and B?
options: Both A and B are compressions
skill: Explain waves and their types
feedback:
The image shows a longitudinal wave in a spring. Both A and B show areas of the spring squashed together. Hence, both A and B are compressions.
A rarefaction is that area of a longitudinal wave where the particles are stretched apart or are farthest from each other.

subject: Science
q: A and B are points on a wave. What are A and B?
skill: Explain waves and their types
feedback:
The figure shows a transverse wave. A is the highest point on the transverse wave called a crest.
And B is the lowest point on the transverse wave called a trough.

subject: Science
q: In a sound wave, the particles of the medium move back and forth, _____ to the direction of the wave. In a violet light wave, the particles of the medium move up and down, _____ to the direction of the wave.
skill: Explain waves and their types
feedback:
Sound waves are longitudinal waves, whereas violet light waves are transverse waves.
In longitudinal waves, the particles of a medium move back and forth, parallel to the direction of the wave.
In transverse waves, the particles of a medium move up and down, perpendicular to the direction of the wave.

subject: Science
q: A _____ combine to form a longitudinal wave. And a _____ combine to form a sound wave.
options: compression and a rarefaction; crest and a trough
crest and a trough; compression and a rarefaction
compression and a rarefaction; compression and a rarefaction
crest and a trough; crest and a trough
skill: Explain waves and their types
feedback:
A compression and a rarefaction combine to form a longitudinal wave.
A sound wave is a type of longitudinal wave and is also made up of a compression and a rarefaction.

subject: Science
q: What is false about sound waves?
options: They are made up of compressions and rarefactions.
They are a type of compressional waves.
The particles in a sound wave move in a direction parallel to the direction of the wave.
skill: Explain waves and their types
feedback: Sound waves are not transverse waves. Sound waves are longitudinal or compressional waves made up of compressions and rarefactions.

subject: Science
q: Sara ties one end of a rope to a tree. She holds the other end of the rope and moves it up and down. What type of waves are produced?
skill: Explain waves and their types
feedback:
As Sara moves the rope up and down, the wave travels away from her hand and towards the tree.
The direction of the movement of the particles is up and down but the direction of the wave is perpendicular to that movement. Therefore, the waves produced are transverse waves.

Longitudinal waves are also called compressional waves and sound waves are a type of longitudinal waves.

subject: Science
q:
Sound waves travel with different speeds as they pass through the four different mediums shown below. Arrange these mediums in the descending order (highest to lowest) of the speed of sound.

1. metal block
2. air
3. oil
skill: Explain the wavelength, frequency and amplitude of sound, and give their units
feedback:
The speed of the sound depends on the type of the medium it passes through. Sound travels fastest in solids, then in liquids and its speed is slowest in the air.

Therefore, sound will travel fastest through the metal block, then in oil, and will be the slowest in the air.

subject: Science
q: The figure below shows green and black waves. What is the frequency of each wave?
options: green wave = 4 Hz; black wave = 2 Hz
green wave = 2 Hz; black wave = 1 Hz
green wave = 2 Hz; black wave = 4 Hz
green wave = 1 Hz; black wave = 2 Hz
skill: Explain the wavelength, frequency and amplitude of sound, and give their units
feedback:
The frequency of a wave is the number of complete waves passing a fixed point in one second.
Green wave: 4 complete waves pass by point 0 in 1 second therefore, the frequency of green wave is 4 Hz.
Green wave: 2 complete waves pass by point 0 in 1 second therefore, the frequency of black wave is 2 Hz.

subject: Science
q: What is true about the orange and the black wave shown below?
options: The orange wave has a lower amplitude than that of the black wave.
The orange wave has a higher frequency than that of the black wave.
The orange wave has a higher amplitude than that of the black wave.
The orange wave has a greater wavelength than that of the black wave.
skill: Explain the wavelength, frequency and amplitude of sound, and give their units
feedback:
The image shows that the orange wave has a greater wavelength and the black wave has a short wavelength.
The amplitude is almost the same for both waves and we cannot determine the frequency unless we know the number of waves passing through a point in one second.

subject: Science
q: The figure shows different points on a wave. Which interval represents one wavelength?
skill: Explain the wavelength, frequency and amplitude of sound, and give their units
feedback:
Wavelength is the distance between a point on one wave and the same point on the next wave. The distance between points C to G represents one wavelength.
A trough and a crest make one complete wave and a wavelength is the length of one complete wave.

subject: Science
q: If the following wave has a frequency of 20 Hz, what is its speed?
skill: Explain the wavelength, frequency and amplitude of sound, and give their units
feedback:
Speed of a Wave = Wavelength x Frequency
The frequency is given as 20 Hz and wavelength is marked in the image as 1.5 m. A crest and a trough make a complete wave.
Speed = 1.5 x 20
Speed = 30 m/s

subject: Science
q: Which letter represents the wave's trough?
skill: Explain the wavelength, frequency and amplitude of sound, and give their units
feedback:
The lowest point on a wave is its trough. D is the lowest point on this wave therefore, point D represents a trough.
Point E represents the highest point on a wave called a crest.

subject: Science

q: Which of the following options best describes the motion of the body shown in this distance-time graph?
options: The body moves at a constantly increasing velocity.

skill: Understand distance-time graphs and solve problems
feedback:

Then we can see that the graph curves upwards between \[10\] and \[15\] seconds, which means that the velocity is increasing. After \[15\] seconds the graph again becomes a straight line. The straight line indicates that the body comes back to moving with a constant velocity.

Remember: in a distance-time graph if a line is straight (which means its slope is constant), the velocity of the body is constant. If the line is curved upwards, it means the velocity is increasing. If the line is curved downwards, it means the velocity is decreasing.

q: When we apply a force on the ground with our feet while walking, the ground applies the same force on our feet, which is the reason we move. Which of the following laws of Newton explains this?
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback: Newton's third law states that every action has an equal but opposite reaction. This also means that every force applied on a body will have an equal but opposite force as well. An example of this law can be found in how humans are able to walk. When we walk, we apply a force on the ground with our feet. The ground applies the same force on our feet but in the opposite direction. It is because of this reaction force of the ground that we are able to move forward. The same can be said about jumping. When we want to jump, we apply a greater force on the ground with our feet. The ground reacts with the same force in the opposite direction, which makes us jump.

Imagine that you and a friend stand on opposite sides of a cart and push the cart using the same amount of force. Will the cart move? It will not move because the resultant or net force of your push will be \[0\] N.

subject: Science
q:
\[ w = mg\]

This equation tells us the weight (\[w\]) of an object if we are given its mass (\[m\]). It is based on which of the following laws of Newton?
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback:
This equation is another way of using Newton's second law of motion. It is used to calculate the weight of an object when we know its mass. \[w\] is the weight which is a force (\[F\]), \[m\] is the mass, \[g\] is the acceleration due to gravity, which is approximately constant on the surface of the Earth.

\begin{align} F &= m \times a \\ w &= m \times g \\ w &= mg \end{align}

subject: Science
q: Every action has an equal but opposite reaction. This statement represents which of the following laws?
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback: Newton's third law of motion states that every action has an equal but opposite reaction. We see this law in action all around us. When a car is moving, its tires push the ground backwards when they are rotating. The ground gives a reaction force, which is equal to the force implemented by the wheel on the ground that pushes the wheel forward. This way, the car moves forward. If you punch a wall, why does your knuckle hurt? The damage should be caused to the wall and not to your knuckle because you are applying force on the wall through your punch. The pain that you feel is because of the reaction force exerted by the wall on your knuckle.

subject: Science
q: When a force is applied on a cart, it accelerates. The acceleration of the cart is directly proportional to _____, and inversely proportional to _____.
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback: The question refers to Newton's second law of motion by using a cart as an example. Newton's second law states that a net force on an object causes it to accelerate. This acceleration is directly proportional to the net force (the greater the force, the greater the acceleration), and inversely proportional to the mass of the object (the greater the mass, the lower the acceleration).

subject: Science
q:
The unit of force N is equivalent to which of the following units?

\[1\] N\[= 1\] _____
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback:
To calculate the units equivalent to the unit of force N, we will use an equation that has force. In this case, we will use the equation derived from Newton's second law of motion:

\[F = ma\]

Next, we will input the units in this equation and then find out what \[1\] N is equal to. The unit for mass (\[m\]) is kg. The unit for acceleration (\[a\]) is ms\[^{-2}\].
\begin{align}

F &= ma \\ 1\mbox{ N} &= 1 (\mbox{ kg})(\mbox{ms}^{-2}) \\ 1\mbox{ N} &= 1 \mbox{ kgms}^{-2} \end{align}

subject: Science
q:
The following proportionality represents which of the given laws?

\[a \propto \frac{F}{m}\]
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback:
Newton's second law of motion states that if a force is applied to a body, it moves with an acceleration that is directly proportional to the magnitude of the applied force.

\[a \propto F\]. The greater the applied force, the higher will be the acceleration.

The law also states that acceleration is inversely proportional to the mass of the object: \[ a \propto \frac{1}{m}\].

The greater the mass, the lower will be the acceleration.

These two parts combined form this proportionality: \[a \propto \frac{F}{m}\]

subject: Science
q: A student tries to lift \[4\] books using the same amount of force as the weight of the books, which acts downwards. What will happen to the books?
options: They will be lowered as they are heavy.
They will fall to the ground.
skill: Describe balanced and unbalanced forces to state and explain Newton's laws of motion and inertia
feedback:
Let us consider the forces that act on the books. The first force is the weight of the books which acts downwards. The second one is the force applied by the student when trying to lift the books; this force acts upwards and is equal to the weight of the books. This means that there are two equal forces acting on the books in opposite directions, therefore, the forces are balanced. There is no net force.



As these two forces cancel each other out, the resultant force will be \[0\] N. This means that the pile of books will stay as it is: it will neither move upward, nor downward.

subject: Science
q:
What is the orbital velocity of the Moon?


Mass of the Moon \[m_{m} = 7.35 \times 10^{22}\] kg
Mass of Earth \[M_e = 6 \times 10^{24}\] kg
Universal gravitational constant \[ G = 6.673 \times 10{-11}\]
skill: Explain motion of satellites, natural and man-made, in relation to Newton's law of gravitation
feedback:
To find the orbital velocity of the Moon we will use the formula for orbital velocity \[v_o\]:

\[ v_o = \sqrt{g_hr_o}\]
But we don't know what the value of \[g_h\], which is the gravity at the height at which the Moon lies. To calculate that, we will have to use the formula for \[g_h\]:

\begin{align} g_h &= G \frac{M_e}{(R+h)^2} \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ R + h = 385{,}400 km = 3.854 \times 10^{8} m\\ \\ &= 0.0027 \mbox{ m/s}^2 \end{align}

Now that we have the value of \[g_h\], we can calculate \[v_o\] of the moon.

\begin{align} v_o &= \sqrt{g_hr_o} \\ &= \sqrt{(0.0027) \times (3.854 \times 10^{8})} \\ &= 1020 \mbox{ m/s} = 1.02 \mbox{ km/s} \end{align}

subject: Science
q:
Calculate the orbital velocity of a satellite that is very close to the surface of Earth.
Radius of Earth \[R = 6.4 \times 10^6\] m.
skill: Explain motion of satellites, natural and man-made, in relation to Newton's law of gravitation
feedback:
We can use the formula for orbital velocity to find the velocities of satellites that are very close to Earth:

\[ v_o = \sqrt{g_hr_o}\]

Because the satellite is really close to Earth, we can approximate the orbital radius to be equal to the radius of Earth. The value of gravity \[g_h\] can also be taken to be the value of gravity on the surface (\[g = 10\] m/s\[^2\]).

\begin{align} v_o &= \sqrt{g_hr_o} \\ &= \sqrt{gR} \\ &= \sqrt{(10) \times (6.4 \times 10^6)} \\ &= 8000 \mbox{ m/s} = 8 \mbox{ km/s} \end{align}

Do not forget to convert your distances from kilometres to metres!

q: What is the velocity of a geostationary satellite with respect to Earth?
skill: Explain motion of satellites, natural and man-made, in relation to Newton's law of gravitation
feedback: A geostationary satellite is a satellite which revolves around the Earth in one day. The Earth also completes its rotation in one day. This means that this satellite stays at the same spot above the Earth as it revolves because the Earth is also rotating with it at the same rate. That is why they are called geoSTATIONARY satellites.

subject: Science
q: Which force keeps satellites engaged in orbit around a planet or star?
skill: Explain motion of satellites, natural and man-made, in relation to Newton's law of gravitation
feedback: We know that when objects revolve around any point or object, there is always a centripetal force acting towards the centre of the revolution. For satellites that revolve around a planet or a star, that centripetal force is actually the force of gravitation between the planet/star and the satellite. Therefore, the force which keeps satellites in orbit is a centripetal force which in this case is the force of gravitational attraction.

subject: Science
q: How long does the Moon take to complete one revolution around Earth?
skill: Explain motion of satellites, natural and man-made, in relation to Newton's law of gravitation
feedback:
The Moon is a natural satellite of the planet Earth. It revolves around Earth and it takes 27.3 days to complete one revolution around Earth. This means that after 27.3 days, the Moon comes back to the point where it had started. The reason we see it almost every day is because of the rotation of the Earth as well as the revolution of the Moon.

Some religions have lunar calendars. For example, one month of the Islamic calendar lasts as long as the revolution of the Moon around Earth: 27.3 days. This is why Islamic months are shorter than the conventional calendar months, which last around 30 days.

subject: Science
q: What are objects that revolve around a planet or star or any astronomical body called?
skill: Explain motion of satellites, natural and man-made, in relation to Newton's law of gravitation
feedback: An object that revolves around a planet or star is called a satellite. Usually it is a planet or a star but objects that revolve around any large astronomical body are called satellites. For instance, the moon is a natural satellite of Earth. Earth is a natural satellite of the sun. Did you know there are moons in our solar system that have their own moons?

subject: Science
q: Which problem was Newton planning to solve when he discovered the idea of gravity?
options: Why do planets revolve around the sun?
Why do apples fall from trees?
Why do boats not sink in water?
skill: Explain motion of satellites, natural and man-made, in relation to Newton's law of gravitation
feedback: While Newton was thinking about why planets revolve around the Sun, he saw an apple fall from a tree. This made him think about why things fall on Earth. Then he started comparing this straight falling motion to the curved motion of a thrown ball falling. He then further imagined that if the ball was thrown hard enough, it would fall around the curve of the Earth. And keep falling. This is how satellites revolve around Earth. They are constantly falling around the curve of the earth and this makes it look like they are revolving around Earth.

subject: Science
q: At what distance from the Earth does its gravitational force of attraction vanish or become \[0\] N?
skill: Explain motion of satellites, natural and man-made, in relation to Newton's law of gravitation
feedback:
The Earth's gravitational force of attraction acts on a body at ALL distances. Therefore, all satellites around Earth experience this force of attraction.

To confirm the answer, put the value of \[F = 0\] N in the given equation for the force of gravitation and calculate the value of \[d\], the distance at which the force will be \[0\].

\[F = G \frac{m_1m_2}{d^2}\]

Remember: if ANYTHING is divided by \[0\], the answer is infinity.

subject: Science
q: A polar satellite is launched at \[850\] km above earth. Find its orbital speed.
skill: Explain motion of satellites, natural and man-made, in relation to Newton's law of gravitation
feedback:
Height \[ = \] h \[ = 850 \] km \[ = 850 \times 1000 \] g \[ = 0.85 \times 10^6 \] m

Orbital velocity v\[_o = ?\]

Orbital velocity 'v\[_o\]' is given as:

v\[_o = \sqrt{ \frac{GM_e}{R + h} } \]

where \[G = \] Universal constant of gravitation \[= 6.673 \times 10^{-11} \] Nm\[^2\]kg\[^{-2}\]

\[M_e = \] mass of the earth \[= 6 \times 10^{24} \] kg

\[R = \] radius of the earth \[ = 6.4 \times 10^6 \]

Putting the values in the equation, we get :

v\[_o = \sqrt{ \frac{ 6.673 \times 10^{-11} \times 6 \times 10^{24} }{6.4 \times 10^6 + 0.85 \times 10^6} } = \sqrt{ \frac{ 40.038 \times 10^{13} }{7.25 \times 10^6} } = \sqrt{5.52 \times 10^7} = \sqrt{55.2 \times 10^6} \]

Hence, v\[_o = 7.431 \times 10^3 = 7431 \] ms\[^{-1}\]
